text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST (PANCREATIC PROTOCOL)
MULTIPHASIC ACQUISITION

CLINICAL INDICATION:
58-year-old female with known pancreatic head/uncinate ductal adenocarcinoma, initially borderline resectable (3.8 cm, SMA contact approximately 180 degrees, SMV abutment). Status post 8 cycles of modified FOLFIRINOX neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Restaging examination. Biliary stent in situ. Prior CA 19-9 at most recent interim restaging (post-cycle 4) was 412 U/mL; at the time of this scan, CA 19-9 is reported as 85 U/mL. Comparison made to prior multiphasic CT abdomen/pelvis (post-cycle 4 restaging) and to the original diagnostic CT.

---

TECHNIQUE:
Multiphasic CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed on a 64-slice multidetector CT scanner following the administration of 120 mL of iohexol intravenous contrast material (Omnipaque 350) at a rate of 4 mL/sec. Acquisitions obtained in the late arterial (pancreatic parenchymal) phase at approximately 40 seconds, portal venous phase at approximately 70 seconds, and delayed phase at approximately 180 seconds post-injection. Thin-section (0.625 mm) axial source images reconstructed in standard and soft tissue kernels; coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations generated. Non-contrast series obtained prior to contrast administration. DLP: 892 mGy-cm.

---

FINDINGS:

PANCREAS:
The pancreatic head/uncinate process mass is again identified as an ill-defined, hypoenhancing soft tissue density relative to the background pancreatic parenchyma, most conspicuous on the pancreatic parenchymal phase images. The lesion has continued to decrease in size and now measures approximately 2.4 x 2.0 cm in axial dimensions (previously 3.1 x 2.7 cm at the post-cycle 4 interim restaging, and originally 3.8 x 3.2 cm at the time of diagnosis), representing continued interval decrease in tumor bulk over the course of neoadjuvant therapy. The margins of the lesion remain indistinct and are accompanied by associated peritumoral desmoplastic change, which limits precise delineation of the lesion boundary.

VASCULAR RELATIONSHIPS:
The relationship of the tumor to the major peripancreatic vessels has markedly improved compared to the initial staging examination and, to a lesser extent, compared to the mid-treatment restaging.

Superior Mesenteric Artery (SMA): The fat plane between the posteroinferior margin of the lesion and the right lateral wall of the SMA is now fully reconstituted on all phases of acquisition. There is no discernible contact between the hypoenhancing mass and the SMA wall on any image set. The SMA is widely patent with normal caliber and branching pattern. This represents a substantial interval improvement from the approximately 180 degrees of tumor-to-artery contact seen at baseline, and further improvement from the approximately 120 degrees of contact noted at the midpoint restaging.

Superior Mesenteric Vein / Portal Vein Confluence (SMV/PV): The SMV, portal vein, and their confluence are clearly delineated with circumferential fat planes fully restored around these structures. There is no imaging evidence of direct tumor abutment or encasement of the SMV, the portal vein confluence, or the splenic vein. No venous deformity, contour irregularity, or intraluminal thrombus is identified. The SMV-portal confluence remains patent with normal venous flow opacification.

Splenic Artery: Intact, with no periarterial soft tissue thickening referable to tumor.

Celiac Axis: Not involved; normal caliber and appearance.

BILIARY SYSTEM:
A covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent is present within the common bile duct and extends into the proximal common hepatic duct, in satisfactory position. The intrahepatic bile ducts are decompressed and non-dilated, with normal intrahepatic biliary caliber throughout. The main pancreatic duct is mildly dilated proximally at approximately 3.5 mm, decreased from the 6 mm dilatation seen at diagnosis, consistent with partial relief of pancreatic ductal obstruction.

LIVER:
The liver is normal in size with smooth contour. No discrete hepatic parenchymal lesions are identified. There are no focal hypodensities or hyperdensities suspicious for metastatic disease on any phase of acquisition. No biliary ductal dilatation within the hepatic parenchyma. No perihepatic fluid collection.

GALLBLADDER:
Gallbladder is present and unremarkable without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid.

LYMPH NODES:
Previously identified peripancreatic lymph nodes have continued to decrease in size. The dominant peripancreatic node, previously measuring up to 0.8 cm at the midpoint restaging (originally up to 1.1 cm), is now not discretely identified as an enlarged node. Several small peripancreatic, periportal, and celiac soft tissue densities remain visible in the expected nodal stations, none measuring above 0.6 cm in short axis. No enlarged retroperitoneal, mesenteric, or pelvic lymph nodes.

PERITONEUM / OMENTUM:
No peritoneal implants. No omental caking or thickening. No ascites. The mesentery appears normal without soft tissue infiltration.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology.

KIDNEYS:
Bilateral kidneys enhance symmetrically with prompt excretion and normal parenchymal thickness. No hydronephrosis, stones, or focal renal masses.

SPLEEN:
Spleen is normal in size and homogeneous in attenuation. No focal splenic lesions.

BOWEL:
No bowel obstruction. No wall thickening or mucosal abnormality. The stomach and duodenum are not distended and appear normal in caliber.

RETROPERITONEUM:
The aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber. No soft tissue mass or adenopathy in the retroperitoneum beyond the peripancreatic nodal changes described above.

PELVIS:
Uterus and adnexa are within normal limits for age. No free fluid or pelvic adenopathy. Urinary bladder is unremarkable.

BONES / SOFT TISSUES:
No lytic or blastic osseous lesions suspicious for metastatic disease are identified in the imaged axial skeleton. No soft tissue masses.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. CONTINUED INTERVAL DECREASE IN PANCREATIC HEAD/UNCINATE MASS: The hypoenhancing pancreatic head/uncinate lesion now measures approximately 2.4 cm, decreased from 3.1 cm at the midpoint restaging and from 3.8 cm at baseline, reflecting an overall size reduction of approximately 37% from the initial diagnostic examination. This is consistent with an ongoing tumor response to neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX chemotherapy.

2. RESOLUTION OF VASCULAR INVOLVEMENT: The previously described circumferential SMA contact (up to 180 degrees at baseline, reduced to approximately 120 degrees at the midpoint) has fully resolved; the fat plane around the SMA is now completely reconstituted. Similarly, the prior SMV-portal confluence abutment with loss of the fat plane has fully resolved, with complete restoration of circumferential fat planes. On the basis of imaging, the tumor no longer meets the vascular criteria previously used to characterize this as borderline resectable.

3. INTERVAL DECREASE IN PERIPANCREATIC LYMPH NODES: The peripancreatic regional lymph nodes have decreased in size and are now within normal limits for size, with no node measuring greater than 0.6 cm.

4. NO HEPATIC METASTASES OR DISTANT DISEASE: No definitive hepatic lesions, peritoneal implants, or other sites of distant metastatic disease are identified.

5. BILIARY STENT IN SATISFACTORY POSITION with decompressed intrahepatic biliary tree and improved pancreatic duct caliber.

The overall imaging picture, in conjunction with the reported CA 19-9 decline to 85 U/mL, supports a favorable tumor response to neoadjuvant therapy. The findings are appropriate for multidisciplinary reassessment regarding resectability and further management planning.

Interpreting Radiologist: [Electronically signed]
Abdominal Imaging Section, Department of Radiology
